title: Stop Making Models Bigger, Make Them Behave — Kobie Crawford, Snorkel
summary: Kobie Crawford demonstrates how a 4B model finetuned with RL to call tools correctly (like `get_table_name`, schema inspection, and self-correction) outperforms a 235B model on SQL query tasks. The approach emphasizes tool discipline over model scale, with training costs under $500 and transfer from single-table to multi-table tasks showing 13.9% to 26.6% improvement on FinQA.
writeup: Kobe Crawford (Snorkel AI) and the UC Berkeley RLLM team demonstrate that a 4-billion-parameter model fine-tuned with reinforcement learning on expert-curated data can outperform a 235-billion-parameter reasoning model on financial tool-use tasks. The central argument is that raw reasoning scale is the wrong solution to the wrong problem: a domain-specific task like SQL-based financial analysis does not require Terence Tao-level general mathematical breadth, and RL is better suited than supervised fine-tuning for reshaping tool-use behavior. The talk grounds the claim in a live failure demo where the 235B model queries a non-existent table twice and then hallucinates an answer, a failure no amount of general reasoning prevented.
key_points: A 235-billion-parameter quantized reasoning model, when asked for the year-over-year YouTube ads revenue growth rate from 2023 to 2024, failed to inspect available database tables, queried a non-existent table twice, and fell back to a hallucinated answer, illustrating that raw reasoning capability does not guarantee disciplined tool use.

The RLLM team frames oversized model selection as the 'Terence Tao effect': deploying a mathematician capable of any domain to do routine SQL queries and arithmetic is a category mismatch, and the domain-specific behavioral discipline a financial analyst needs is not the same as general brilliance.

Reinforcement learning is chosen over supervised fine-tuning specifically because the target improvement is behavioral, shaping how the model chooses and sequences tool calls rather than injecting new factual knowledge into its weights.

Snorkel's data-generation process uses PhD-level domain experts and industry practitioners in the loop, combined with a verification stage that confirms each task has a reliably queryable answer, making data quality the primary lever rather than model scale.

Enterprise production drivers for the small-model approach include inference cost, latency, on-premise deployment feasibility, and data-control compliance requirements in regulated sectors such as finance and healthcare, all of which are undercut by dependence on a 235B hosted model.

chapters.title: RL Approach and Expert Data Pipeline
